original_abstract:
Memories do not emerge in a vacuum; they take shape in direct dialogue with the narratives that preceded them. Consequently, memory activism involves more than just bringing suppressed histories to light—it requires the active "decommissioning" of hegemonic narratives that have long dominated the public sphere. This article explores how public monuments, as massive material anchors of ideology, have become central flashpoints for protest and regime change. 

The very physical qualities that allow monuments to impose authority—their scale, permanence, and central location—also make them potent platforms for dissent. By examining the "agentic capacity" of these objects, the study surveys various strategies used to strip toxic monuments of their normative power. These range from radical demolition and museum relocation to creative "re-signifying" through guerrilla art and counter-monumentalism. Ultimately, the paper argues that while dismantling the old is essential for clearing a "space of appearance" for new actors, it must be balanced against the risk of erasing historical traces that facilitate necessary public debate.
humanized_abstract:
Our memories do not emerge in a historical vacuum; they are always conversing with other stories that have been told. This is why memory activism often involves not only un-covering or un-telling the histories that have been erased but also decommissioning or ‘pulling down’ the official narratives that have been dominant. As permanent, physical symbols of dominant ideology, monuments have become focal points of struggle and a trigger for revolutionary change. This anthology article provides a comprehensive and critical look at monuments and their place in struggles over history and democracy.

How can you fight a monument with such evident bodily characteristics as height, solidity and centrality, capable of overwhelming those who encounter it and exercising influence over the surrounding space? It is possible to look at the “agentic capacity” of these objects, to examine the different strategies for depriving the “toxic monument” of its everyday efficacy. These include their definitive destruction, their transfer to museums or other sites, and their re-signifying through acts of guerrilla art and counter-monumentality. But, however important it is to destroy the old in order to create an empty space of appearance open to the emergence of new political actors, this process should not hinder the continuing political function of the monument as a forum for discussion of historical events of public relevance.
